description: Climate change refers to the long-term warming of the planet, which is primarily caused by human activities such as burning fossil fuels, deforestation, and certain agricultural and industrial practices. This warming is driven by the increasing levels of greenhouse gases in the Earth's atmosphere, which trap heat and lead to rising global temperatures. The consequences of climate change are far-reaching and include more frequent and severe weather events, rising sea levels, and disruptions to ecosystems and biodiversity.

nodes.label: Climate Change
nodes.description: The long-term warming of the planet, primarily caused by human activities
nodes.fun_fact: The term 'climate change' was first used in the 1970s

nodes.label: Greenhouse Effect
nodes.description: The process by which certain gases in the Earth's atmosphere trap heat, leading to warming
nodes.fun_fact: The greenhouse effect is a natural process that has been occurring for millions of years

nodes.label: Renewable Energy
nodes.description: Energy sources that are sustainable and do not contribute to climate change, such as solar and wind power
nodes.fun_fact: Renewable energy sources now account for over 30% of global electricity generation

nodes.label: Sustainable Development
nodes.description: The practice of meeting the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs
nodes.fun_fact: The concept of sustainable development was first introduced in the 1980s

nodes.label: Ocean Acidification
nodes.description: The decrease in the pH of the ocean due to the absorption of carbon dioxide from the atmosphere
nodes.fun_fact: Ocean acidification can have devastating effects on marine ecosystems, particularly coral reefs

nodes.label: Ecosystem Conservation
nodes.description: The practice of protecting and preserving ecosystems and the biodiversity they support
nodes.fun_fact: Ecosystem conservation is critical for maintaining the health of the planet and supporting human well-being

nodes.label: IPCC
nodes.description: The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change, which provides scientific guidance on climate change
nodes.fun_fact: The IPCC was established in 1988 by the United Nations

nodes.label: El Niño
nodes.description: A complex weather pattern that occurs when the surface temperature of the Pacific Ocean warms up
nodes.fun_fact: El Niño events can have significant impacts on global climate patterns

nodes.label: Orangutan
nodes.description: A species of great ape that is threatened by climate change
nodes.fun_fact: Orangutans are one of the most endangered species in the world

nodes.label: James Hansen
nodes.description: A climate scientist who has made significant contributions to our understanding of climate change
nodes.fun_fact: James Hansen is often referred to as the 'father of climate change awareness'

nodes.label: Carbon Dioxide
nodes.description: A greenhouse gas that is released through human activities such as burning fossil fuels
nodes.fun_fact: Carbon dioxide levels have increased by over 40% since the Industrial Revolution

nodes.label: Fossil Fuels
nodes.description: Energy sources such as coal, oil, and gas that are extracted from the earth and contribute to climate change
nodes.fun_fact: Fossil fuels have been the primary source of energy for human societies for over a century

nodes.label: Deforestation
nodes.description: The clearance of forests, usually as a result of human activities such as agriculture or urbanization
nodes.fun_fact: Deforestation accounts for around 15% of global greenhouse gas emissions

nodes.label: Industrial Revolution
nodes.description: A period of significant economic and technological change that occurred in the 18th and 19th centuries
nodes.fun_fact: The Industrial Revolution marked the beginning of the widespread use of fossil fuels

nodes.label: Climate Change 2007: The Physical Science Basis
nodes.description: A report published by the IPCC that provides an assessment of the physical science basis of climate change
nodes.fun_fact: This report was a major milestone in the development of climate change science

edges.description: The greenhouse effect is a key component of climate change
Renewable energy is a alternative to fossil fuels and can help mitigate climate change
Sustainable development is a key aspect of addressing climate change
Climate change can lead to ocean acidification
Ecosystem conservation is critical for addressing climate change
Carbon dioxide is a greenhouse gas that contributes to the greenhouse effect
Renewable energy is a alternative to fossil fuels
Deforestation is a aspect of unsustainable development
Ocean acidification can have devastating effects on marine ecosystems, including orangutans
Ecosystem conservation can help protect orangutans and other species
The IPCC published the report 'Climate Change 2007: The Physical Science Basis'
El Niño is a complex weather pattern that can contribute to climate change
James Hansen has made significant contributions to our understanding of climate change
Carbon dioxide is released through human activities such as burning fossil fuels
Fossil fuels emerged as a primary source of energy during the Industrial Revolution
Deforestation occurred during the Industrial Revolution
The Industrial Revolution contributed to the emergence of climate change

key_takeaways: Climate change is a complex and multifaceted issue that requires a comprehensive approach
The greenhouse effect is a key component of climate change, and carbon dioxide is a major contributor to this effect
Renewable energy and sustainable development are critical for addressing climate change
real_world_impact: Climate change is having a significant impact on the world, from more frequent and severe weather events to rising sea levels and disruptions to ecosystems and biodiversity. The consequences of climate change are far-reaching and will require a coordinated and sustained effort to address. The transition to renewable energy sources, the protection of ecosystems, and the promotion of sustainable development are all critical components of this effort. By working together, we can mitigate the effects of climate change and create a more sustainable future for all.

images.title: 20200118 Global warming and climate change - vertical block diagram - causes effects feedback.png
images.description:
Block diagram indicating primary causes and wide-ranging effects, including feedback, of global warming and climate change.
Of course, other elements and causal paths (arrowed connecting lines) could have been included, but are omitted to keep the diagram reasonably simple for a lay audience.

Contrasting global and regional climatic variability, by comparing global average temperature changes with temperature changes in the Caribbean region.
